item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The way I see it, the Foundation wasn't sure exactly what it was before the events of Addendum A. After Hostile Takeover gets more information on the entity, the Foundation pieces together the current description from what they have. SCP-3532's tweets about followers both imply it had some sort of following in the past, and when it worked with the marketing company the accounts and contract were filled with religious terminology. Even still, the Foundation isn't one hundred percent sure on what's making the posts, which is why the description says they believe it was worshipped as a god, and recovered files suggest it lives in some other plane.

Thank you for the feedback and I'm glad you enjoyed the article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was thinking this was the SCP version of the Slumbering Prince at first, and I was a little disappointed. But I'm pleased to say it stands well on its own, regardless of comparison.

how Twitty why the gods katyperry and potus contain more follow

Relatable.

That said, I agree that I'm not sure why this is listed as being a former deity in the description; it seems like it should be taken as just an anomalous Twitter account that posts cognitohazards. Then the story of who this guy is and why he wants followers can come out via all the addenda and so forth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the second social anomaly cyberbullied into submission by the foundation.
When will this violence stop?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, I hadn't even heard of it until just now. It seems pretty interesting though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was there any inspiration taken from "American Gods" while writing this SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The reason he's not Neutralized is because he still exists out there in the ether somewhere. SCP-3532 could still pop up at anytime in the future, it's just really unlikely due to the Foundation crushing both his spirits and public image.

Thanks for reading, I'm glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
please lord cena tall me

This article kinda made me sad. Some kid (who happens to be a god) just wants more followers. Instead, he gets stabbed in the back by his idol. Also, shouldn't his object class be Neutralized, if he stopped posting? (Or not, if he's still out there?)
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, I've made some edits that hopefully resolve these issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the core idea here is pretty good - an old god trying to use social media to become popular/worshipped again and being utterly clueless at it - but I have a few problems with the article.

For starters, it just feels kind of bloated - lots of extraneous detail on the specific social media managers and its company. I feel like you could trim this down a lot without losing anything. It could also use another editing pass; you've also got a few missing words or weird word choices, especially early on in the article (I didn't make a list, sorry).

My other issue is how they are dealing with this thing in the first place. They think its some kind of god, but based on what? Besides the documents they got from the agency, they don't seem to have any source of knowledge for what it is, and don't seem to make any effort to actually find it and put it into containment - surely this agency representing it would have its address or something? You say its a god and then treat it as if its some purely digital entity that can't be contained.

As I said, I think you've got a decent idea here, but I think it still needs some work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice article. Minor correction, the first paragraph of Description has "pubic" where it should be "public".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New addition to my favourites. +1. I'll keep following you, SCP-3532.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for reading! I've noticed the line spacing too, it bugged me a lot while writing. I think it's just Wikidot weirdness at play, since I could never really get it to go away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Within two months, the number of visitors to SCP-3532 run accounts had dropped by 95%.

holy crud

Good article! Line spacing is a bit strange at the end paragraphs, might want to look into that

item.title: SCP-3532
item.content:encoded: My first SCP!! A big thank you to everyone who read through and critiqued the numerous versions of this!
